description: UActorComponent, USceneComponent, and the composition model — when to use components vs inheritance

entries.title: Components vs Inheritance: When to Use Each
entries.description: Unreal favors composition over inheritance. Instead of creating deep class hierarchies, you attach reusable Components to Actors. Understanding when to create a component vs a new Actor subclass is one of the most important architectural decisions in UE5.
entries.whatItIs: Components are modular pieces of functionality that you add to Actors. An Actor is a container — Components give it behavior, visuals, collision, and logic. This is the 'composition over inheritance' pattern that UE5 is built around.
whenToUse: When the behavior needs to be reused across different Actor types (e.g., a health system used by players, enemies, and destructibles)
When you want to add or remove functionality at runtime (e.g., attaching a shield component when the player picks up armor)
When the logic doesn't need its own world transform or collision — use UActorComponent
When the logic needs a position in the world (attachable to sockets, has transform) — use USceneComponent
When designers need to mix and match behaviors on Actors via Blueprints without code changes
whenNotToUse: When the behavior IS the entity — a player character should subclass ACharacter, not be a component on a generic AActor
When you need to override core Actor lifecycle behavior like Tick priority or replication rules — that's inheritance
When the 'component' would need to spawn other Actors or manage world state — consider a Subsystem or standalone Actor instead
When you only have one Actor type that will ever use this logic — a component adds indirection for no benefit
commonMistakes: Creating a deep class hierarchy (AEnemy -> ARangedEnemy -> ARangedEnemyWithShield) instead of using components (AEnemy + UShieldComponent + URangedAttackComponent)
Using USceneComponent when you don't need a transform — UActorComponent is lighter and signals intent better
Forgetting to call RegisterComponent() when creating components outside of the constructor
Not using CreateDefaultSubobject<T>() in constructors — using NewObject<T>() for components in constructors won't register them properly
Putting component logic in the owning Actor instead of in the component itself, defeating the purpose of composition
keyPoints: UActorComponent: no transform, no attachment. Pure logic (health system, inventory, ability manager).
USceneComponent: has transform, can attach to sockets and other components. Base for all visible/spatial components.
CreateDefaultSubobject<T>() in constructor to create default components that always exist on the Actor.
RegisterComponent() + AddInstanceComponent() when creating components at runtime (outside constructor).
Components can Tick independently — each has its own PrimaryComponentTick settings.
One Actor can have many components. Components can reference each other via GetOwner()->FindComponentByClass<T>().
Think of Actors as 'what it is' (enemy, pickup, door) and Components as 'what it can do' (take damage, be interacted with, move).
entries.lifecycle: Constructor (CreateDefaultSubobject) -> OnRegister -> InitializeComponent (bWantsInitializeComponent=true) -> BeginPlay -> TickComponent -> EndPlay -> OnUnregister -> DestroyComponent

entries.title: Common Built-in Components
entries.description: UE5 ships with dozens of ready-made components. Knowing which ones exist saves you from reinventing the wheel.
entries.whatItIs: A quick reference to the most commonly used built-in components in UE5 and what they do.
whenToUse: When setting up a new Actor and choosing which components to add
When looking for engine-provided functionality before writing custom logic
When deciding between a built-in component and a custom one
whenNotToUse: These are building blocks, not complete systems — you'll usually extend or combine them
commonMistakes: Using UStaticMeshComponent for animated meshes — use USkeletalMeshComponent instead
Adding UCharacterMovementComponent to a non-ACharacter — it requires ACharacter specifically
Forgetting that USpringArmComponent needs a child component (usually Camera) attached to it to work
Using UChildActorComponent for things that should be regular components — ChildActor has replication and lifecycle overhead
keyPoints: Movement: UCharacterMovementComponent (characters), UFloatingPawnMovement (flying), UProjectileMovementComponent (projectiles), URotatingMovementComponent (spinning objects)
Audio: UAudioComponent for spatialized 3D sound attached to an Actor
UI: UWidgetComponent to display a UMG widget in 3D world space (health bars, interaction prompts)
Utility: UArrowComponent (editor visualization), UChildActorComponent (spawn another Actor as a 'component')
Splines: USplineComponent for paths, roads, and rails
